Facts.text: St Gregory II begins his reign as Catholic Pope
The high altar of Paris cathedral Notre Dame is consecrated by Cardinal Henri de Château-Marçay and Maurice de Sully
Philip of Burgundy is installed as Bishop of Utrecht by Charles, Duke of Burgundy
Battle of Raichur: Krishnadevaraya, ruler of the Indian Vijayanagara Empire successfully takes the fort of Raichur from the Sultanate of Bijapu, with 132,00 troops and 550 elephants
Capitulation of Wittenberg: John Frederick I, Protestant Elector of Saxony, resigns after his capture by Catholic Hapsburg forces of Charles V at the Battle of Mühlberg,
Miguel Lopez de Lagazpi founds Manilla in the Philippines
Spain confiscates English ships in Spanish harbors - beginning of the Anglo-Spanish War
Matthias von Habsburg's army reaches Lieben, at Prague
France's First Minister, Cardinal Richelieu declares war on Spain (Franco-Spanish War 1635–59)
Battle at Rocroi/Allersheim: French forces destroy a Spanish army
Dutch expedition to southern Chile led by Hendrik Brouwer captures Spanish-held islands of Chiloë and massacres its garrison
Massachusetts Bay, Plymouth, Connecticut, and New Haven form the United Colonies of New England
England is declared a Commonwealth by an act of the Rump Parliament, making England a republic for the next 11 years
Spanish troops occupy Grevelingen
At about midday, near-total darkness descends on New England, now known to be caused by forest fires in Canada
Russian army enters Poland
Netherlands captures French island of St Maarten (held until 1795)
Game protection law restricts encroachment on Indian hunting grounds
French Order of Legion d'Honneur forms
Senator Charles Sumner of Massachesetts speaks out against slavery
Americans William Francis Channing and Moses G. Farmer patent the electric fire alarm
Siege of Vicksburg, investment of city complete
Battle of Port Walthall Junction, Virginia (Bermuda Hundred)
Last engagement in series of battles known as Spotsylvania
Skirmish at Cassville, Georgia
Blanche Kelso Bruce appointed register of treasury by President Garfield
William F. “Buffalo Bill” Cody opens Buffalo Bill's Wild West show in Omaha, Nebraska
Ringling Brothers circus premieres in Baraboo, Wisconsin
First mass production of shoes by Jan Matzeliger's Consolidated Lasting Machine Company in Lynn, Massachusetts
Rice Institute is chartered in Houston, Texas, becomes Rice University
American engineer Charles Brady King invents the pneumatic hammer
National Society of Colonial Dames of America founded
Heavy rain washes "quick clay" into a deep valley killing 111 in Norway
US Congress passes the Private Mailing Card Act, allowing private publishers and printers to produce postcards, which have to be labeled "Private Mailing Cards" until 1901, known as "souvenir cards"
Great Britain and Boers resume peace talks in Pretoria
Tom Jenkins beats Frank Gotcha for heavyweight wrestling champ
Federated Boys' Club (Boys' Club of America) organizes
Italian King Victor Emmanuel and Swiss President Ludwig Forrer open the Simplon Tunnel, a railroad tunnel through the Alps that is the world's longest until 1982
Passage of Earth through tail of Halley's Comet causes near-panic
Webb Alien Land-Holding Bill passes, forbidding Japanese from owning land
Washington's 1st Sunday game, Senators beat Cleveland 1-0 in 18 innings
Kelud volcano on Java, erupts killing 5,160
Mount Kelud in Java, Indonesia erupts, resulting in one of the most deadly eruptions of the 20th century and killing an estimated 5,000 people in 104 small villages
US Congress sharply curbs immigration, setting a national quota system
KPD (communist revolts) in German Ruhr cities occupied by Allies
French air force bombs Damascus, Syria
National Broadcasting Company (NBC) founded by the Radio Corporation of America (RCA)
"Firedamp" explodes in Mather coal mine, Pennsylvania, killing 195 of 273 miners
51 frogs enter 1st annual "Frog Jumping Jubilee" (Angel's Camp, California)
Cloudburst causes stampede in Yankee Stadium, crushes 2 people to death
White women win voting rights in South Africa
Cruiser Deutschland launched in Kiel
Military coup by Colonel Damian Veltsjev in Bulgaria
Sherlock Holmes crossword puzzle in "Saturday Review of Literature"; males who solved puzzle became members of Baker Street Irregulars
NFL adopts an annual college draft to begin in 1936
Amsterdam time becomes MET (Middle European Time)
Germany occupiers in Holland forbid bicycle taxis
New Nazi battleship Bismarck leaves Gdynia, Poland
Braves Paul Waner is 3rd NLer to get 3,000 hits (Anson & Wagner)
Berlin is declared "Judenrien" (free of Jews)
Churchill pledges Britain's full support to US against Japan
German defense line in Italy collapses
Start of the 1st Victory Test Cricket between England & Aust Services
Dutch Cooperation for Sexual Reform (NVSH) forms in Amsterdam
NY Times reports of worlds smallest & dumbest mechanical brain
Nuclear explosion in Nevada (fall-out in St George, Utah)
US House of Representatives votes to ratify the state constitution and admit Ohio to the Union retroactively as of March 1, 1803, 150  years after overlooking the paperwork
Postmaster General Summerfield approves CIA mail-opening project
Atkinson & Depeiaza make 347 stand for 7th wkt WI v Australia
Pachyderm Building at Cleveland Metroparks Zoo opens (Cleveland, Ohio)
Pittsburgh Pirates Dale Long hits 9th-inning HR, 1st HR in 8 straight games
Adone Zoli forms Italian government
Jan de Quay becomes premier of the Netherlands
The USS Triton, the first submarine with two nuclear reactors, is completed
Alan Freed & eight other radio DJs accused of taking radio payola (bribes from record companies)
Belgian parliament requires rest day for self employed
USAF Major Robert M. White takes X-15 to 33,222 m
New pier opens in Scheveningen, the Netherlands
Soviet dancer Rudolf Nureyev makes his Paris stage debut with the Kirov Ballet
"John Birch Society" single by Chad Mitchell Trio hits #99
Indonesian paratroopers land in New Guinea
US performs nuclear test at Christmas Island (atmospheric)
US diplomats find at least 40 secret microphones in Moscow embassy
Patricia R. Harris named 1st US black female ambassador (Luxembourg)
USSR ratifies treaty with Britain & US banning nuclear weapons in space
20th Emmy Awards: "Get Smart", "Mission Impossible" & Barbara Bain win
Frank Howard fails to homer, after hitting 10 in 6 consecutive games
Pirate Radio Brumble of Northern England 1st heard
USSR launches Mars 2, becomes the 1st spacecraft to crash land on Mars

25th Cannes Film Festival: "The Working Class Goes to Heaven" directed by Elio Petri and "The Mattei Affair" directed by Francesco Rosi jointly awarded the Grand Prix du Festival International du Film
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
WMAV TV channel 18 in Oxford, MS (PBS) begins broadcasting
Sentimental ballad "Daisy A Day" single by Jud Strunk hits #14

Stanley Cup Final, The Spectrum, Philadelphia, PA: Goaltender Bernie Parent and the Flyers shut out the Boston Bruins, 1-0, to win series 4-2, become 1st "expansion" team to win Stanley Cup; Parent named playoff MVP
Valeri Giscard d'Estaing wins French presidential election
Farm truck packed with wedding party struck by a train, killing 66 in truck, 40 miles south of Poona, India
Gold ownership legalized in Australia
Senate establishes permanent Select Committee on Intelligence
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
"In The Navy" single by Village People hits #3
Ringo &Barbara Bachare involved in a car crash
Five British Army soldiers are killed when their armoured vehicle is ripped apart by a Provisional Irish Republican Army roadside bomb near Bessbrook, County Armagh
MLB Pittsburgh Pirates Jim Bibby gives up a leadoff single to Atlanta Braves Terry Harper, then retires next 27 batters
36th Cannes Film Festival: "Narayama Bushiko" (The Ballad of Narayama ) directed by Japanese director Shohei Imamura wins the Palme d'Or
NASA launches Intelsat V satellite, no. 506
Weird Al Yankovicgives live performance at Wax Museum in Washington, D.C.
Pat LaFontainescores two goals in 22 seconds in an NHL playoff game
STS 41-D vehicle moves to launch pad
39th Cannes Film Festival: "The Mission" directed by Roland Joffe wins the Palme d'Or
Anti-apartheid activist Hélène Pastoors sentenced to 10 yrs in South Africa
40th Cannes Film Festival: "Sous le soleil de Satan" directed by Maurice Pialat wins the Palme d'Or
Dow Jones Industrial Average passes 2,500 mark for 1st time, closes at 2,501.1
Sue Ellen's (Linda Gray) last appearance on TV show "Dallas"
Willy T. Ribbs overcomes numerous mechanical problems in qualifying to become the first African-American driver to make the Indianapolis 500 starting grid
27th Amendment ratified, prohibits Congress from raising its salary
Englishman Dave Gauder, 224 lbs, pulls 196 ton jumbo jet, 3 inches
US VP Dan Quayle attacks Murphy Brown for being a single mother and as a poor example of family values
Boeing 727 crashes into mountain at Medellín, Colombia, kills 132
Dow Jones Industrial Average closes above 3,500 for 1st time (3,500.03)
Final episode of American TV drama "LA Law" after 8 year run
World's youngest doctor, Balamurali Ambati, 17, graduates from Mount Sinai
STS 77 (Endeavour 11), launches into orbit
Andrew Motion is appointed British Poet Laureate for 10 years, the first to request a definite term
Manchester United lose 3-1 to Tottenham at White Hart Lane but win English Premier League title for the 3rd consecutive season
TV political comedy "The Thick of It" created by Armando Iannucci, starring Peter Capaldi and Chris Langham premieres on BBC 4
Rock for the Rainforest benefit concert held at Carnegie Hall, NYC; performers include: Sting, James Taylor,Billy Joel, Sheryl Crow, and Lenny Kravitz
In an all South African final the Bulls (Pretoria) edge the home team Sharks, 20-19 in Durban for their first Super 14 Rugby title; Bulls flyhalf Derick Hougaard boots 2 penalties & 2 conversions
Sri Lanka announces victory in its 25-year war against the terrorist organization, Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam

Decorated Dutch coach Louis van Gaal confirmed as manager of Manchester United; Ryan Giggs named assistant, confirms retirement as a player at age 40 after 963 games and English record 22 major trophies
UK inflation is recorded as a negative for the first time since 1960
EgyptAir flight MS804 goes missing over the Mediterranean on route Paris to Cairo
71st Cannes Film Festival: Japanese film "Shoplifters" by Hirokazu Kore-eda wins the Palme d'Or
Sprinter Dutee Chand comes out as India's first openly gay professional athlete
US billionaire Robert F. Smith announces he will pay off college loans of nearly 400 students of the graduating class of Morehouse College, Atlanta
Greenhouse gas emissions dropped 17% worldwide in April 2020 when world was in lockdown, in study published in "Nature Climate Change"
Two dams on Tittabawassee River in central Michigan breached by floodwaters, forcing evacuation of thousands of residents
Argentine President Alberto Fernandez announces a 'circuit-breaker lockdown' as the country records over 39,000 daily COVID-19 cases
BBC investigation finds their 1995 Princess Dianna interview was secured through 'deceitful behavior' by journalist Martin Bashir
New York City begins re-opening after 423 days (March 2020) with the COVID-19 death toll at 33,000
New York Yankees pitcher Cory Kluber no-hits Texas Rangers, 2-0 at Globe Life Field, Arlington, Texas
The EU agrees to open its borders to vaccinated travelers from safe countries
Argentine landmark criminal trial finds state responsible for massacre of more than 400 Qom and Moqoit people in 1924 in the Chaco region. First trial of its kind in Latin America.
Environmental pollution caused by “obesogen" toxins is having a significant effect on the obesity crisis and can be passed down through the generations according to major new study
Mercedes confirms the sale of world's most expensive car - a 1955 Mercedes-Benz SLR coupe for €135 million ($142 million)
Oklahoma passes a bill banning nearly all abortions, the most restrictive in the country
Pop-R&B vocal group the Spinners donate nearly 400 stage outfits to the Motown Museum in Detroit, Michigan
Attempted Coup in Congo foiled after a group attacks the President's Palace in Kinshasa
Iran's President Ebrahim Raisi dies in a helicopter crash in foggy conditions, along with his foreign minister Hossein Amir-Abdollahian and other officials
Manchester City beats West Ham 3-1 at Etihad Stadium to become the first team to win the English Premier League title for 4 consecutive seasons
Sean "Diddy" Combs issues an apology after a video is released by CNN of him assaulting his ex-girlfriend in 2016, after previously denying it happened
Syzygy: Eight of nine planets are forecast to align on the same side of the Sun
Christopher Columbusselects his son Diego Columbus as sole heir
George Duke of Saxony sells Friesland for 100,000 gold guilders to Charles, Duke of Burgundy (later EmperorCharles V)
French explorerJacques Cartierleaves France on his second voyage of discovery to Newfoundland for Francis I, with three ships
EnglishQueen Elizabeth IarrestsMary, Queen of Scots
British KingGeorge IIof England grants charter to Ohio Company to settle Ohio Valley in America
U.S. PresidentJohn Quincy Adamssigns the Tariff of 1828/Tariff of Abominations into law to protect industry in the North
German ChancellorOtto von Bismarcktakes possession of Cameroon and Togoland
Camille Saint-Saëns' 3rd Symphony in C premieres at St. James Hall, London, with the composer conducting
1st automobile made byKarl Benz, arrives in Netherlands
PlaywrightOscar Wildeis released from Reading Gaol
Portugal's KingCarlos Inames João Franco prime minister
In his first title defenseJack JohnsonfightsPhiladelphia Jack O'Briento a no decision in 6 rounds in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania to retain his world heavyweight crown
Maurice Ravel's opera "L'heure espagnole" premieres at the Opéra-Comique in Paris
AL PresidentBan Johnsontells Detroit Tigers if they continue protest ofTy Cobb's suspension, they will be banned from baseball
Mustafa Kemal Atatürklands at Samsun on the Black Sea coast, beginning the Turkish War of Independence
Chinese warlord Feng Yuxiang declares war onChiang Kai-Shek's nationalist government
English Cardinal John Fisher and statesmanThomas More, both executed byHenry VIII, canonized as saints by PopePius XI
WWII: French counterattack at Péronne under GeneralCharles De Gaulle
240 gypsies transported toAuschwitzfrom Westerbork, Netherlands, including Sinti girl Settela Steinbach famously filmed by Jewish photographer Rudolf Breslauer
Writer and civil rights activistW.E.B. Du Boisbecomes 1st Black member of The American Academy of Arts and Letters
"Time" magazine features 'Theseus', an electrical learning machine built by American scientistClaude Shannon- the 1st example of AI
Premiere ofHarold Pinter's play "Birthday Party" in London
Rodgers and Hammerstein's "South Pacific" soundtrack album goes #1 and stays #1 for 31 weeks
SF Giants rookieJuan Marichalbeats Philadelphia Phillies, 2-0 on debut at Candlestick Park; first National League hurler since 1900 to debut with a one-hitter
Marilyn Monroesings "Happy Birthday, Mr. President" toJFKbefore 15,000 attendees, accompanied by jazz pianist Hank Jones, at Madison Square Garden, NYC
Stan MusialbreaksHonus Wagner's NL hit record with 3,431
27th Emmy Awards: "TheMary Tyler MooreShow", Robert Blake, and Jean Marsh win
Columbia Records releases "Turnstiles", singer-songwriterBilly Joel's fourth studio album
Caper comedy film "Smokey and the Bandit" starringBurt Reynolds,Sally FieldandJackie Gleasonpremieres at Radio City Music Hall in NYC
ActressSophia Lorenis jailed for tax evasion in Naples, Italy
Stanley Cup Final, Northlands Coliseum, Edmonton, AL:Wayne Gretzkyscores twice as Edmonton Oilers beat NY Islanders, 5-2 for a 4-1 series win; Oilers first SC title
Carlos LehderRivas, of Colombia's Medellin drug cartel, is convicted in Florida for smuggling more than 3 tons of cocaine into US
Red Sox retireBobby Doerr's #1
"Do the Right Thing", directed bySpike Lee, starring Danny Aiello and Ossie Davis premieres at the Cannes Film Festival
"Elvis" TV drama series about early life ofElvis Presleylast airs on ABC
"Some Gave All" debut album byBilly Ray Cyrusis released (Billboard Album of the Year 1993)
Amy Fishershoots Mary Jo Buttafuoco in the face in an altercation over an affair with her husband, in Massapequa, New York
American tennis starJennifer Capriati(18), checks into a drug rehab center
Egyptian actorOmar Sharifsuffers a mild heart attack
"The Lost World:Jurassic Park", directed bySteven Spielberg, and starringJeff GoldblumandJulianne Moore, premieres in the US
RapperDMXreleases his debut studio album"It's Dark and Hell Is Hot", debuts at No. 1 Billboard 2000 Chart
"Star Wars: Episode I - The Phantom Menace", directed byGeorge Lucas, starringEwan McGregor,Natalie PortmanandLiam Neeson, is released in cinemas
"Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End", directed by Gore Verbinksi, starringJohnny Depp,Keira KnightleyandOrlando Bloompremieres in Anaheim - most expensive film ($300 million) made at the time
"Sicko" a documentary on the US health care system, directed byMichael Moorehas its premiere at the Cannes Film festival.
Coen brothers film "No Country for Old Men", based on theCormac McCarthynovel, starringTommy Lee Jones,Javier BardemandJosh Brolinpremieres at the Cannes Film Festival (Best Picture 2008)
TV musical show "Glee" starringJane Lynch, Michael Morrison,Lea Micheleand an ensemble cast premieres on Fox
Alpha Magnetic Spectrometer, project to search for dark matter, led bySamuel C. C. Ting, installed on the International Space Station
Historic first handshake betweenPrince Charlesand Sinn Féin leaderGerry Adamsat the National University of Ireland in Galway
American actressMeghan MarklemarriesPrince Harryat a ceremony in Windsor Castle, making her a member of the British royal family
Queen Elizabeth IIgrants the titles of Duke and Duchess of Sussex toPrince HarryandMeghan Markleupon their marriage
Owl/Legacy Recordings releases "Seven Psalms", the fifteenth solo studio album by American singer-songwriterPaul Simon
Police in Paris, France discover statue of "Doors" singerJim Morrisonthat had been stolen in 1988 from his gravesite at Père-Lachaise Cemetery
Public unveiling of Titian's masterpiece "Assumption of the Virgin" a painted altarpiece in the Basilica di Santa Maria Gloriosa dei Frari, Venice
Anne Boleyn, second wife of English King Henry VIII, is beheaded at the Tower of London on charges of adultery, incest and treason
Act of Uniformity comes into effect, re-introducing a revised Book of Common Prayer after the Restoration
Giovanni della Robbia, Italian Renaissance sculptor (The Resurrection of Christ, Ospedale del Ceppo at Pistoia), born in Florence, Republic of Florence (d. c. 1529)[1]
Isabella d'Este, Marquise of Mantua, born in Ferrara, Duchy of Ferrara (d. 1539)
Johann Jakob Froberger, German singer, organist, and composer, baptized in Stuttgart, Duchy of Württemberg (d. 1667)
José de Escandón, Spanish colonizer and the first governor of the Nuevo Santander province, born in Soto la Marina, Spain (d. 1770)
Augustus Hervey, 3rd Earl of Bristol, British politician and admiral known as a "Naval Casanova", born in England (d. 1779)
Joseph Franz Weigl, Bavarian-Austrian cellist and early proponent of the 'thumb technique', born in  Duchy of Bavaria (d. 1820)
Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz, Queen of England, consort to King George III1, born in Unteres Schloß, Mirow, Duchy of Mecklenburg-Strelitz, Holy Roman Empire (now Germany),  (d. 1818)
Johann Friedrich Peter, Dutch-born American composer, born in Heerendijk, Holland (d. 1813)
Gabriele Prota, Italian composer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1843)
Johann Gottlieb Fichte, German philosopher (Wissenschaftslehre), born in Rammenau, Electorate of Saxony (d. 1814)
Rahel Varnhagen von Ense-Levin, German literary hostess, born in Berlin, Prussia (d. 1833)
Arthur Aikin, British mineralogist and founding member of the Geological Society, born in Lancashire, England (d. 1854)
Johns Hopkins, American financier and philanthropist (founded Johns Hopkins Hospital and Johns Hopkins University), born in Gambrills, Maryland (d. 1873)
Maria Isabel of Braganza, Queen of Spain, born in the Palace of Queluz, Portugal (d. 1818)[1]
Sarah Miriam Peale, American portrait painter (General Lafayette, 1825), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1885)[1]
Samuel Jameson Gholson, American jurist and Brigadier General in the Confederate Army, born in Madison County, Kentucky (d. 1883)
Felix Zollicoffer, American journalist and Confederate Brigadier General who was killed in the Battle of Mill Springs, born in Maury County, Tennessee (d. 1862)
Catherine Dickens, British author, cook book novelist (What Shall We Have For Dinner?), and wife of Charles Dickens (1836-58), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1879)
John Gross Barnard, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Sheffield, Massachusetts (d. 1882)
Paul-Armand Challemel-Lacour, French politician, historian and ambassador to Switzerland (1879) and London (1880-82), born in Avranches, France (d. 1896)[1]
Adin Ballou Underwood, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Worcester County Massachusetts (d. 1888)
Alice Mary Smith, British composer, born in London (d. 1884)
John Jacob Abel, American pharmacologist (endocrine glands), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1938)
Roland Napoléon Bonaparte, French prince and president of the Société de Géographie, born in Paris, France (d. 1924)
Vittorio Emanuele Orlando, Prime Minister of Italy (1917-19) who represented Italy at the 1919 Paris Peace Conference, born in Palermo, Kingdom of the Two Sicilies (d. 1952)
Carl Akeley, American taxidermist and naturalist who developed the animal mount process and other techniques for museums, born in Clarendon, New York (d. 1926)

John Fillmore Hayford, American Geodesist who established the theory of isostasy and founded the modern science of geodesy (precise measurements of the shape of the earth), born in Rouses Point, New York (d. 1925)
Henry Horatio Dixon, Irish Biologist who originated the cohesion-tension theory of water and mineral movement in plants, born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1953)
Albert Fish, American serial killer and cannibal, born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1936)
Nishida Kitarō, Japanese philosopher (Kyoto School), born in Unoke, Japan (d. 1945)
Reginald Aldworth Daly, Canadian-American Geologist who independently developed the theory of magmatic stopping, born in Napanee, Ontario (d. 1957)
Federico Gerdes, Peruvian pianist, composer, and educator (founder and first director of the National Conservatory of Music, 1909-26), born in Tacna, Peru (d. 1953)
Gilbert Jessop, English cricket batsman (18 Tests; Wisden Cricketer of the Year 1898), born in Cheltenham, England (d. 1955)
William King Gregory, American zoologist and paleontologist (mammalian teeth, evolutionary theory), born in Greenwich Village New York (d. 1970)
Adam Carse, English composer and instrument collector, born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1958)
Theodore Goddard, British attorney, born in  Highbury, London (d.    1952)
Henricus W. J. M. Keuls, Dutch lawyer and poet (Dancing Lamp), born in Obdam, Netherlands (d. 1968)
Arthur Meulemans, Flemish composer, conductor, and music teacher (Adriaen Brouwer), born in Aarschot, Belgium (d. 1966)
Oswald Boelcke, German World War I pilot and author of the first manual of air combat (Dicta Boelcke), born in Giebichenstein, German Empire (d. 1916)[1]
George "Pops" Foster, American jazz double bassist, tuba player, and trumpeter (Louis Armstrong; Sidney Bechet; Earl Hines), born in Macoll, Louisiana (d. 1969)
Albert Hay Malotte, American pianist, organist, composer (Disney staff, 1930's; The Lord's Prayer), and educator, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1964)
Cecil Gray, Scottish composer and music critic, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1959)
Michael Balcon, English film producer (head of Ealing Studios), born in Birmingham, England (d. 1977)
Frank Luke Jr., American World War I pilot with 19 aerial victories and the first US pilot to receive the Medal of Honor, born in Phoenix, Arizona (d. 1918)
Jerome Travers, American golfer (US Open 1915), born in New York City (d. 1951)
Julius Evola, Italian far-right philosopher and fascist writer (Ride the Tiger), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1974)
Manuel Ivo Cruz, Portuguese composer and founder of Philharmonic Orchestra of Lisbon, born in Corumbá, Brazil (d. 1985)
Anthony Bushell, British actor (Journey's End, A Night to Remember), born in Kent, England (d. 1997)
Sven Thofelt, Swedish athlete (Olympic gold pentathlon 1928; silver team épée 1936, bronze 1948), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1993)
Bruce Bennett, American athlete and actor (Before I Hang, Sahara), born in Tacoma, Washington (d. 2007)
Gerd Bucerius, German lawyer, newspaper publisher (Die Zeit, 1946-95), and politician, born in Hamm, Germany (d. 1995)
John Griggs, American actor (The Joey Bishop Show; Fearless Fosdick), born in Evanston, Illinois (d. 1967)
Percy Williams, Canadian athlete (Olympic gold 100m / 200m 1928), born in Vancouver, British Columbia (d. 1982)
Schlomo Joffe, Polish-Israeli folk and classical musician and composer, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1995)
Sir Nicholas Winton, British humanitarian ('British Schindler' - saved 669 children from Nazis), born in London (d. 2015)[1][2]
Alan Melville, South African cricket batsman (11 Tests, 4 x 100, HS 189; Natal, Oxford University CC, Sussex CCC, Transvaal), born in Carnarvon, South Africa (d. 1983)
Bert Hardy, English photographer (Picture Post), born in London (d. 1995)
Sanjiva Reddy, Indian politician, sixth President of India (1977-82), born in Illur, British India (d. 1996)
Go Seigen [Wu Qingyuan], Chinese-born Japanese Go master considered the greatest Go player in the 20th century, born in Minhou County, Fujian, Republic of China (d. 2014)
Max Perutz, Austrian-British molecular biologist and recipient of the Nobel Prize in Chemistry (1962), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2002)
Abraham Pais, Dutch-American physicist and science historian (Subtle is the Lord: The Science and the Life of Albert Einstein), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2000)[1][2]
Mitja Ribičič, Italian-born Slovenian communist politician and Prime Minister of Yugoslavia (1969-71), born in Trieste, Italy (d. 2013)
Tina Strobos, Dutch physician and resistor to Nazi occupation, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2012)
Daniel Gélin, French actor (Obsession, The Man Who Knew Too Much), born in Angers, Maine-et-Loire (d. 2002)
Karel van het Reve, Dutch writer, translator, and Russian literature historian (Comrade's Religion), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1999)
William H. Stewart, 10th US Surgeon General (1965-69), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 2008)
David McLean [Eugene Joseph Huth], American actor known for appearing in Marlboro advertisements (Days of Our Lives, Tate), born in Akron, Ohio (d. 1995)
(Alexander) "Sandy" Wilson, British theatrical composer and lyricist (The Boy Friend; Divorce Me, Darling!), born in Sale, Cheshire, England (d. 2014)
David Jacobs, British broadcaster (Juke Box Jury), born in London (d. 2013)
Paul Cooper, American classical music composer, and educator, born in Victoria, Illinois (d. 1996)
Swami Kriyananda [J. Donald Walters], American Hindu guru, yoga teacher, and author, born in Teleajen, Romania (d. 2013)
John Thompson, American football executive (GM Seattle Seahawks 1976-82; NFL Executive of the Year 1978), born in South Bend, Washington (d. 2022)
Cees Slinger, Dutch jazz pianist, composer and bandleader, born in Alkmaar, Netherlands (d. 2007)
Curt Simmons, American baseball pitcher (3 x MLB All-Star; World Series 1964 St. Louis Cardinals; Philadelphia Phillies), born in Whitehall Township, Pennsylvania (d. 2022)
Harvey Cox, American theologian (The Secular City; The Seduction of the Spirit), and educator (Harvard Divinity School, 1965-2009), born in Phoenixville, Pennsylvania
John Stroger American politician (Cook County (Illinois) Board of Commissioners, 1970-2006), born in Helena, Arkansas (d. 2008)
Mavis Rivers, Samoan-New Zealand jazz singer, born in Apia, Samoa (d. 1992)[1]
Michael Adamis, Greek electroacoustic music composer, born in Piraeus, Greece (d. 2013)
Hans Kox, Dutch composer (Dorian Gray; Symphony No. 4 - "Tasmanian"), and educator, born in Arnhem, Netherlands (d. 2019)
Lorraine Hansberry, American playwright (A Raisin in the Sun), and writer (To Be Young Gifted And Black), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1965)
Bob Anderson, British motorcycle road racer and racing driver, born in London (d. 1967)
Eric Davidson, English screenwriter (Look, Mike Yarwood!), born in Deptford, England (d. 1996)
Éric Tappy, Swiss operatic tenor, born in Lausanne, Switzerland (d. 2024)
Ruben Radica, Croatian classical and electronic music composer (Towards A), and educator, born in Split, Yugoslavia (d. 2021)

Trevor Peacock, British stage and screen actor (The Vicar of Dibley - "Jim Trott"), screenwriter, and songwriter ("Mrs. Brown, You've Got A Lovely Daughter"), born in Edmonton, Middlesex, England (d. 2021)
Alma Cogan, British pop singer ("Dreamboat"; "I Can't Tell a Waltz from a Tango"), born in Whitechapel, London, England (d. 1966)

Bill Fitch, American Basketball HOF coach (NBA C'ship 1981, Boston Celtics;NBA Coachof the Year 1976, 80; NBA All-Star coach 1982; Cleveland Cavaliers; NJ Nets, Houston Rockets, LA Clippers), born in Davenport, Iowa (d. 2022)
Claude Blanchard, French Canadian singer, comedian and actor, born in Joliette, Canada (d. 2006)
Daniel Kramer, American portrait photographer (album covers for Bob Dylan, 1965), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2024)[1]
Paul Erdman, Canadian-American economist and author, born in Stratford, Ontario, Canada (d. 2007)
James A. Abrahamson, American Air Force general, astronaut Director of Reagan's Strategic Defense Initiative and businessman, born in Williston, North Dakota
David Sinclair, British actor (Love & Hate; My Partner, the Ghost), born in Elham, England (d. 1996)
Jan Wijn, Dutch classical pianist and pedagogue, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2022)
Jim Lehrer, American television news anchor (McNeil-Lehrer Report/PBS Newshour, 1975-2011), and novelist, born in Wichita, Kansas (d. 2020)
Monica Getz (née Silfverskiold), Swedish-American advocate for addiction treatment and divorce law reform (Coalition for Family Justice), born in Sweden (d. 2025)[1]
Ruskin Bond, Indian novelist (Our Trees Still Grow in Dehra), born in Kasauli, British India
